title: AI Delegation Orchestration: From Conversation to Durable Agent Work
thesis: AI interfaces can stay conversational, but consequential AI work should be governed through bounded delegations, durable delegation records, operator control surfaces, and domain-specific review boundaries instead of relying on chat transcripts as the system of record.
primary_reader: Builders, researchers, advanced AI users, and operators of multi-agent or long-running AI workflows who are starting to feel the limits of chat-session-based work.
intended_outcome: Readers should understand the distinction between conversational input and delegated work, then move through a practical series covering delegation records, operator cockpits, control loci, long-running delegations, capability contracts, and commitment boundaries in high-stakes domains.
artifact_fit: The topic is a cross-disciplinary design argument that connects HCI, coordination theory, agent tooling, governance, and domain practice. It is better suited to a series than one article because each layer needs examples, diagrams, and cautions.
smallest_viable_version: A seven-part draft series with a source-controlled visual plan: conversation-to-delegation, delegation record schema, operator cockpit, control loci, long-running delegations, capability contracts, and commitment boundaries in high-stakes domains.
scope_risks: The main risk is redundancy across articles. The series handles this by assigning each article one reader question and using bridges rather than repeating the full framework every time.
alternatives_considered: A single comprehensive essay was rejected because it would bury the core idea under too many adjacent topics. A pure tooling survey was rejected because the durable contribution is a design primitive, not a product roundup.
claims: Natural language can remain the main interaction modality for AI while durable work state lives outside the chat transcript.
A chat transcript is a weak system of record for consequential AI work because it does not reliably preserve scope, constraints, state, evidence, review, or stop conditions.
Consequential multi-step AI work should be represented as bounded delegations with explicit objectives, non-goals, control boundaries, evidence, risks, and exit criteria.
Not every AI interaction should become a delegation; quick explanations, brainstorming, and disposable drafts can remain lightweight conversations.
The series should support both conceptual and technical readers by pairing technical terms with analogies, diagrams, and concrete scenarios.

Operator-level orchestration should route decisions to executor agents, verifier agents, arbiter agents, policy rules, context-refresh capabilities, or humans depending on the decision, rather than defaulting every uncertainty to the human.
The broader article series should treat operator cockpits, control loci, long-running delegations, capability contracts, and commitment boundaries in high-stakes domains as separate follow-up topics.

sources.title: Malone and Crowston, The Interdisciplinary Study of Coordination
Endsley, Toward a Theory of Situation Awareness in Dynamic Systems
Gutwin and Greenberg, A Descriptive Framework of Workspace Awareness for Real-Time Groupware
Mark, Gudith, and Klocke, The Cost of Interrupted Work
ABA Formal Opinion 512, Generative Artificial Intelligence Tools

sanitized_summary: This proposal is now a seven-part series arguing that AI work should be split into conversational interaction and durable delegated state. It introduces delegation records, operator cockpits, control loci, long-running delegation loops, capability contracts, and commitment boundaries in high-stakes domains using generic examples only.

abstraction_examples.original: An operator runs several AI work sessions and later struggles to recover what each session did and what instruction should come next.
abstraction_examples.abstracted: An operator supervises several active AI delegations and needs a durable state record to resume, compare, and route next actions.

abstraction_examples.original: An orchestration system uses domain-specific capabilities and review policies to decide when agents should continue without human input.
abstraction_examples.abstracted: A capability network routes work among executor, verifier, arbiter, policy, and human review surfaces according to a delegation record.

abstraction_examples.original: A specific project's coding agent is told not to touch billing, authentication, or schema files while fixing a checkout issue.
abstraction_examples.abstracted: A coding delegation includes explicit non-goals and forbidden action boundaries while allowing reversible branch-local edits.

agent_involvement: The proposal was created by an AI agent from a sanitized research discussion and public sources; human review is required before public publication.
